coaching.coaching: Plan how shared test file creation and ownership across stack will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: shared test file creation and ownership across stack

coaching.coaching: Generalize unenumerated routing-map callers in code-review directory across the rest of the work.
coaching.subject: unenumerated routing-map callers in code-review directory

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py` (and the synthetic-repo fixture + `project.foo` attribution it introduces) will exist before this ticket's work begins, or explicitly note that this ticket is responsible for creating the file when the dependency is unavailable.
evidence: Plan states: 'tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py — extend this shared test file (a new file first created earlier in the stack; this ticket appends its Pass-3 routing cases)'
Grep of entire tests/ tree for 'test_code_review_project_criteria' returns zero matches
The dependency ticket `rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a` referenced as child 2 could not be resolved (ticket not found)
findings.finding: The plan describes `tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py` as 'a new file first created earlier in the stack' and states this ticket 'appends its Pass-3 routing cases' to it — but the file does not exist anywhere in the test tree. If the prior stack ticket has not yet landed (or the plan misstates which ticket creates it), the implementation work here has no file to append to, making the Testing and AC sections unexecutable as written.
scenarios: The prior stack ticket that was supposed to create the file has not yet landed; the implementer has no file to append to and no fixture definitions (synthetic-repo fixture, logical `project.foo` attribution) available.
The plan's AC selector `-k routing` targets a file that doesn't exist yet, so `pytest` would silently collect zero tests and report a false-green.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit ordering note: 'This ticket MAY create `test_code_review_project_criteria.py` if the dependency ticket has not merged; otherwise it appends.' Alternatively, reference the exact commit/PR that introduces the file so reviewers can verify the dependency is already merged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the relationship label: if this ticket cannot start until rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a is complete, call it a 'prerequisite' (or 'dependency'), not a 'child', to avoid sequencing confusion.
evidence: Scope says 'This ticket **depends on** child 2 [rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a]' — a dependency on a 'child' inverts the normal parent→child direction

Testing section reinforces the dependency: 'The routed finding carries the logical `project.foo` id that child 2 [rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a] will attribute' — confirming this ticket cannot be implemented until rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a is done, yet it is labeled a child
findings.finding: The plan calls rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a a 'child' ticket while simultaneously declaring this ticket 'depends on' it — these labels contradict each other about sequencing/hierarchy direction, which would confuse an implementer about which ticket must be completed first.
scenarios: An implementer reads 'child' and assumes this ticket is the prerequisite, begins implementing without the synthetic-repo fixture and logical id attribution that rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a provides, causing test failures.
A project manager sequences this ticket before rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a based on the 'child' label.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'child 2 [rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a]' with 'prerequisite ticket [rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a]' (or 'blocker') in both the Scope and Testing sections, so the dependency direction is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Approach section explaining *why* computing `effective_routing` once at function entry and binding it via `functools.partial` is the right fit — specifically that `ThresholdResolver` is a 1-arg protocol and `partial` is the correct adapter without touching the kernel or registry signatures.
evidence: Approach section contains: 'Pure wiring — thread the repository-effective routing map into the two Pass-3 resolvers' — this is mechanical description, not rationale.

Positive rationale appears only in: 'the shared resolvers already exist and are the single source of truth; duplicating them would diverge from plan-review's routing semantics' — framed as anti-duplication argument under 'Rejected alternative', not as an affirmative fitness statement.

ABSENCE: no statement in the Approach section explains *why* computing `routing` once at function entry and binding via `functools.partial` is the correct pattern given that `ThresholdResolver` is a 1-arg protocol.
findings.finding: The Approach section describes the mechanism but does not independently state a positive rationale for *why* `functools.partial` + a single pre-computed `effective_routing` call is the right structural choice (the only 'why' is buried in the rejected-alternative blurb as a negative: 'duplicating them would diverge'). An implementer reading only the Approach narrative sees the *what* but not the affirmative *why-this-fits*.
scenarios: An implementer might choose a different adapter pattern (e.g., a lambda closure, or an inner def) without understanding that partial is idiomatic precisely because ThresholdResolver is a typed 1-arg Callable — minor but leaves the rationale implicit.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach paragraph, e.g.: 'Computing `routing` once at function entry and binding it via `functools.partial` is the correct adapter because `ThresholdResolver` is typed as a single-argument callable (`Sequence[str] -> tuple[float, bool]`); this keeps the kernel and registry signatures unchanged and confines all routing-awareness to the workflow step that owns repo context.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `import functools` (or an equivalent lambda-based binding) must be added to `workflow_ops.py` as part of the `functools.partial` wiring described in the Approach.
evidence: `workflow_ops.py` imports: `import logging` and `from typing import Any` only — confirmed by file read of lines 15-22
Plan text: 'functools.partial(registry.threshold_for, routing_map=routing)' — `functools` is not imported in `workflow_ops.py`
Alternative: plan could specify a local lambda instead of `functools.partial`, which would need no new import
findings.finding: `workflow_ops.py` does not currently import `functools`, yet the plan prescribes `functools.partial(registry.threshold_for, routing_map=routing)` without specifying that `import functools` must be added. This is a concrete, provable structural gap: the implementation will raise `NameError: name 'functools' is not defined` at runtime if the import is omitted.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Bind that map into the `threshold_for` callable handed to `pass3_over_findings` (e.g. `functools.partial(registry.threshold_for, routing_map=routing)`)'
scenarios: Implementer follows the plan verbatim, adds the `functools.partial` call, but omits `import functools` — the step raises `NameError` at first invocation of `code_review_decide`
Reviewer approves the diff without noticing the missing import because the plan doesn't flag it as a required change
findings.suggested_fix: Add `import functools` to the imports block in `workflow_ops.py` alongside `import logging`, or revise the Approach to use a lambda: `lambda criteria: registry.threshold_for(criteria, routing_map=routing)` — which requires no new import and is equally clear.

findings.finding: The plan asserts the synthetic-repo fixture (and the `test_code_review_project_criteria.py` shared file) have already been 'landed' by child story rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a, but that ticket's own dep-manifest records 'dep absent tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py' and its status is 'open' — the file does not exist in the repo.
findings.location: Testing section / 'reuses child 2's synthetic-repo fixture, whose finding now carries logical `project.foo` via the fan-in landed there [rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a]'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard dependency on rebar:761e-5af2-ed57-408a, or explicitly scope the test section to say: 'if 761e-5af2-ed57-408a has not landed, create `test_code_review_project_criteria.py` from scratch with the synthetic-repo fixture described in that ticket's Testing section.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (or fix) that `detectors.py:138` calls `registry.threshold_for([crit])` without a `routing_map`, leaving detector blocking_enabled decisions unaffected by project overlays after this ticket; either note it as a deferred seam or include it in scope.
evidence: Plan scope states only: 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py — thread effective_routing(ctx.repo_root) into the Pass-3 threshold_for binding and nit_suppressed_criteria call'
detectors.py:138: 'blocking_enabled = registry.threshold_for([crit])[1]' — no routing_map arg, will keep using packaged default
Plan does not mention detectors.py or apply_failclosed as an out-of-scope callsite
Plan claims 'Third of five seams' but does not name detectors.py as a deferred seam
findings.finding: detectors.py:138 calls registry.threshold_for([crit]) with no routing_map — an unenumerated caller in the code_review directory whose blocking decisions will continue to use the packaged-default routing even after this ticket lands, meaning a project overlay that re-tunes blocking_enabled for a detector criterion won't be respected in apply_failclosed.
scenarios: A project overlay sets blocking_enabled: false for a detector criterion; apply_failclosed still reads the packaged default (blocking_enabled: true) and blocks the review unexpectedly.
A project overlay sets blocking_enabled: true for a non-default detector criterion; the detector blocking still uses the packaged default.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Scope section noting that detectors.py:138 (apply_failclosed) is a separate unrouted caller and is intentionally deferred as a later seam, so reviewers know the omission is deliberate.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
